lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Why, so: now have I done a good day’s work:

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: You peers, continue this united league:

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: I every day expect an embassage

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: From my Redeemer to redeem me hence;

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: And now in peace my soul shall part to heaven,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Since I have set my friends at peace on earth.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Rivers and Hastings, take each other’s hand;

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Dissemble not your hatred, swear your love.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: By heaven, my heart is purged from grudging hate;

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: And with my hand I seal my true heart’s love.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: So thrive I, as I truly swear the like!

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Take heed you dally not before your king;

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Lest he that is the supreme King of kings

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Confound your hidden falsehood, and award

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Either of you to be the other’s end.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: So prosper I, as I swear perfect love!

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: And I, as I love Hastings with my heart!

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Madam, yourself are not exempt in this,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Nor your son Dorset, Buckingham, nor you;

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: You have been factious one against the other.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Wife, love Lord Hastings, let him kiss your hand;

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: And what you do, do it unfeignedly.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Here, Hastings; I will never more remember

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Our former hatred, so thrive I and mine!

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Dorset, embrace him; Hastings, love lord marquess.

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: This interchange of love, I here protest,

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: Upon my part shall be unviolable.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And so swear I, my lord. They embrace.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Now, princely Buckingham, seal thou this league

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: With thy embracements to my wife’s allies,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: And make me happy in your unity.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Whenever Buckingham doth turn his hate

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: On you or yours to the Queen, but with all duteous love

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Doth cherish you and yours, God punish me

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: With hate in those where I expect most love!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: When I have most need to employ a friend,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And most assured that he is a friend,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Deep, hollow, treacherous, and full of guile,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Be he unto me! this do I beg of God,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: When I am cold in zeal to yours. They embrace.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: A pleasing cordial, princely Buckingham,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Is this thy vow unto my sickly heart.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: There wanteth now our brother Gloucester here,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: To make the perfect period of this peace.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And, in good time, here comes the noble duke.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Good morrow to my sovereign king and queen;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And, princely peers, a happy time of day!

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Happy, indeed, as we have spent the day.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Brother, we have done deeds of charity;

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Made peace of enmity, fair love of hate,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Between these swelling wrong-incensed peers.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: A blessed labour, my most sovereign liege:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Amongst this princely heap, if any here,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By false intelligence, or wrong surmise,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Hold me a foe;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If I unwittingly, or in my rage,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Have aught committed that is hardly borne

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By any in this presence, I desire

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To reconcile me to his friendly peace:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: ’Tis death to me to be at enmity;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I hate it, and desire all good men’s love.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: First, madam, I entreat true peace of you,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which I will purchase with my duteous service;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Of you, my noble cousin Buckingham,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If ever any grudge were lodged between us;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Of you, Lord Rivers, and, Lord Grey, of you;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That all without desert have frown’d on me;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Dukes, earls, lords, gentlemen; indeed, of all.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I do not know that Englishman alive

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: With whom my soul is any jot at odds

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: More than the infant that is born to-night:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I thank my God for my humility.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: A holy day shall this be kept hereafter:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I would to God all strifes were well compounded.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: My sovereign liege, I do beseech your majesty

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To take our brother Clarence to your grace.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Why, madam, have I offer’d love for this,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To be so flouted in this royal presence?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Who knows not that the noble duke is dead? They all start.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: You do him injury to scorn his corse.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Who knows not he is dead! who knows he is?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: All-seeing heaven, what a world is this!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Look I so pale, Lord Dorset, as the rest?

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: Ay, my good lord; and no one in this presence

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: But his red colour hath forsook his cheeks.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Is Clarence dead? the order was reversed.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But he, poor soul, by your first order died,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And that a winged Mercury did bear;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Some tardy cripple bore the countermand,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That came too lag to see him buried.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: God grant that some, less noble and less loyal,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nearer in bloody thoughts, but not in blood,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Deserve not worse than wretched Clarence did,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And yet go current from suspicion!

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: A boon, my sovereign, for my service done!

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: I pray thee, peace: my soul is full of sorrow.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: I will not rise, unless your highness grant.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Then speak at once what is it thou demand’st.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: The forfeit, sovereign, of my servant’s life;

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Who slew to-day a righteous gentleman

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Lately attendant on the Duke of Norfolk.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Have I a tongue to doom my brother’s death,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: And shall the same give pardon to a slave?

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: My brother slew no man; his fault was thought,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: And yet his punishment was cruel death.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Who sued to me for him? who, in my rage,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Kneel’d at my feet, and bade me be advised?

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Who spake of brotherhood? who spake of love?

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Who told me how the poor soul did forsake

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: The mighty Warwick, and did fight for me?

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Who told me, in the field by Tewksbury,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: When Oxford had me down, he rescued me,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: And said, “Dear brother, live, and be a king”?

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Who told me, when we both lay in the field

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Frozen almost to death, how he did lap me

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Even in his own garments, and gave himself,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: All thin and naked, to the numb cold night?

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: All this from my remembrance brutish wrath

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Sinfully pluck’d, and not a man of you

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Had so much grace to put it in my mind.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: But when your carters or your waiting-vassals

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Have done a drunken slaughter, and defaced

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: The precious image of our dear Redeemer,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: You straight are on your knees for pardon, pardon;

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: And I, unjustly too, must grant it you:

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: But for my brother not a man would speak,

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Nor I, ungracious, speak unto myself

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: For him, poor soul. The proudest of you all

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Have been beholding to him in his life;

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Yet none of you would once plead for his life.

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: O God, I fear thy justice will take hold

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: On me, and you, and mine, and yours for this!

lines.speaker: KING EDWARD
lines.text: Come, Hastings, help me to my closet. Oh, poor Clarence!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: This is the fruit of rashness! Mark’d you not

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: How that the guilty kindred of the queen

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Look’d pale when they did hear of Clarence’ death?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: O, they did urge it still unto the king!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: God will revenge it. But come, let us in,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To comfort Edward with our company.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: We wait upon your grace.

stage_directions.text: Flourish. Enter King Edward sick, Queen Elizabeth, Dorset, Rivers, Hastings, Buckingham, Grey, and others.
Exeunt some with King and Queen.

glosses.definition: formal message or summons (by an ambassador)
Christ — Edward anticipates death
do not disguise your hatred; admit it and swear it off
God (1 Timothy 6:15, Revelation 17:14, 19:16)
destroy, ruin
in opposition; belonging to rival factions
unviolable — unable to be broken
confirm this alliance (as one seals a legal document)
complete ending; perfect conclusion — 'period' in the old rhetorical sense
royal overlord; formal address to the king
Elizabeth's brother and son — named together; marked for Pomfret
Gloucester announces Clarence's murder to a court that believed him reprieved
Edward had countermanded the death warrant — too late
the fleet messenger-god — carried the first (fatal) order swiftly
slow lame messenger — Gloucester's cruel image of the reprieve
too late; lagging behind
pass (like a current coin) unsuspected
the life-forfeit; the death penalty owed
pronounce my brother's death-sentence
the Earl of Oxford unhorsed Edward at Tewkesbury; Clarence rescued him
wrap me
beastly rage
cart-drivers; low-class workmen

glosses.phrase: precious image of our dear Redeemer
glosses.definition: the human body as the image of God — Genesis 1:26–27; Edward's theological framing of murder
help me to my private chamber — closet = small retreat-room

annotations.title: The sick-king reconciliation, staged as theatre
annotations.body: Edward's deathbed peace-making is one of the play's most overtly theatrical set-pieces: each peer is walked to another, hands are joined, and formal vows of love are spoken before the king. Edward insists on performance ("Dissemble not your hatred"), but the whole court knows the gestures are hollow. The audience knows more — we have watched Richard engineer Clarence's imprisonment and death in 1.1 and 1.3. The scene thus functions as a false reconciliation, a ritual that cannot undo the murder already accomplished offstage. Buckingham's vow ("When I have most need to employ a friend, / And most assured that he is a friend, / Deep, hollow, treacherous, and full of guile, / Be he unto me!") is the play's most explicit instance of dramatic irony: Buckingham swears against the exact fate he will meet at Richard's hands in Act V.
sources: Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

annotations.title: Clarence's death announced, and the craft of the counter-blame
annotations.body: When Richard tells the assembled court "Who knows not that the noble duke is dead?" he has already pre-positioned the accusation: the king's first (fatal) order was obeyed by a winged Mercury; the countermand was carried by a tardy cripple. The image is Richard's rhetorical signature — he externalizes responsibility onto the speed of messengers rather than his own actions. Elizabeth's horrified "All-seeing heaven, what a world is this!" is the only response available to characters who do not yet understand they are watching a murderer script their future. The scene's closing couplet from Gloucester ("God will revenge it") is the play's most brazen hypocrisy — Richard invokes divine justice for a death he himself commissioned.
sources: A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)

annotations.title: Edward's self-indictment: "Who sued to me for him?"
annotations.body: Edward IV's speech — "My brother slew no man; his fault was thought" — is one of the play's unexpected moments of genuine feeling. Edward recalls the field at Tewkesbury when Clarence rescued him from Oxford, recalls lying frozen under Clarence's own cloak. The speech mourns not only Clarence but his own failure as king and brother: no one in his court spoke for Clarence; his royal wrath overrode his memory. What makes the speech dramatically strong is its structural position — Edward gets this moment of clarity, then dies offstage, never to speak again. The play's political machinery has used up the one man capable of honouring the bonds of kinship. Richard will inherit a court trained to stay silent.

annotations.title: "God will revenge it" — Richard's theological irony
annotations.body: Richard's closing aside — "God will revenge it" — uses divine retribution as deflection. The line is characteristically double-voiced: at the level of plot it is a cover-story (the queen's kin will be blamed for Clarence's death); at the level of the play's larger theology it is an admission. God does revenge it, in 5.3, when the ghosts arrive. Richard the plot-mover speaks words that the play's moral order will make true against him. This is the pattern Shakespeare learned from the medieval morality plays and is now refining: the villain, speaking glibly, utters the judgement that will eventually come for him. Whether or not Shakespeare shares the theology, the dramaturgy requires it: Richard's doom must be prefigured in his own speech.
sources: Bernard Spivack, Shakespeare and the Allegory of Evil (1958)
E. M. W. Tillyard, Shakespeare's History Plays (1944)

trials.prompt: Why does Edward IV stage the reconciliation among his peers?
options: He expects to go on a long journey and wants peace behind him
He expects to die soon and wants his court at peace before his death
He has received a vision commanding it
He is preparing to abdicate in favour of Gloucester

trials.prompt: How does Richard explain why Clarence's reprieve failed to reach him in time?
options: That the king's written order was lost in transit
That a "winged Mercury" bore the first (fatal) order and a "tardy cripple" bore the countermand, arriving too late
That the jailer willfully disobeyed the reprieve
That the queen's allies intercepted the reprieve

trials.prompt: Buckingham's oath against those who would turn on him — the elaborate self-curse at 32–40 — functions as what?
options: A sincere pledge of loyalty
Dramatic irony: he unwittingly predicts the exact fate he will meet at Richard's hands in Act V
A legal binding that makes the reconciliation permanent
A ceremonial formality with no narrative consequence

trials.prompt: What emotional register does Edward's speech (102–132) bring that the play has not yet sustained?
options: Comic irony and wordplay
Grieved clarity — genuine remorse, specific memory of Tewkesbury, and self-indictment as king and brother
Satirical mockery of the court
A prophetic vision of future events

trials.prompt: When Richard says "God will revenge it" after Clarence's death is announced, the line is an example of what?
options: Genuine piety
Deflection that is also an unwitting prophecy — God will revenge it, against Richard, in 5.3
A stage direction mistaken as dialogue
A scriptural quotation from the Book of Job
